title: Discovering Data Encoding Strategies for Quantum-Classical Neural Networks Using Monte Carlo Tree Search
authors: Lena Tokuhiro, Amine Bentellis, Jeanette Miriam Lorenz
abstract: Quantum machine learning (QML) has attracted considerable research interest, yet whether it offers practical benefits over classical approaches remains an open question. The choice of data encoding significantly influences QML performance, but why certain encodings outperform others remains poorly understood. We employ Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to discover optimal data encoding circuits for a quantum-classical convolutional neural network (QCCNN) combining a non-variational quantum block for feature extraction with a classical classifier. Evaluating on two medical imaging datasets, the discovered circuits outperform commonly used encoding strategies while showing competitive results compared to purely classical counterparts. We further analyze metrics to identify predictors of encoding performance. Entanglement capability and Fourier decomposition provide minimal insight, whereas the effective rank of the feature maps exhibits meaningful correlation and can serve as a threshold criterion to accelerate the search for high-performing encodings. Our findings provide both a practical method for encoding discovery and new insights into what makes data encodings effective in QML.
summary: This paper uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to automatically find high-performing quantum data encoding circuits for hybrid neural networks. It discovers that the diversity of the feature map, measured by effective rank, is a key predictor of success, providing a way to accelerate the search for effective quantum machine learning models.
main_problem: The challenge of discovering optimal data encoding strategies for Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Networks (QCCNNs) and identifying which circuit properties predict classification performance.
main_result: MCTS-discovered encoding circuits outperform standard strategies, and the effective rank of feature maps was identified as a strong predictor of encoding performance, whereas entanglement and Fourier properties were not.
method: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with progressive widening is used to automate the discovery of encoding circuits, combined with an analysis of feature map diversity via effective rank.
model_or_system: A hybrid Quantum-Classical Convolutional Neural Network (QCCNN) architecture using non-variational quantum blocks for feature extraction on medical imaging datasets (BreastMNIST and PneumoniaMNIST).
key_observables: Classification accuracy, Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), effective rank of feature maps, entanglement capability, and Fourier spectrum.
important_parameters: Patch sizes (2x2 and 3x3 qubits), scaling factor (f), and MCTS exploration constant (c).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a noise-free simulation regime and does not explicitly account for NISQ-era hardware noise or classical simulation hardness.
figures_summary: Schematic of QCCNN architecture, visual representation of discovered circuits, performance comparisons against baseline encodings, training metrics (loss/accuracy), and correlations between effective rank and AUC.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QCCNN problem, describes the MCTS-based search methodology, presents the discovered circuits and their performance on medical datasets, analyzes the correlation between circuit metrics and accuracy, and discusses limitations regarding noise and quantum advantage.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Architecture sketch of the CNN and QCCNN for the patch size 2 × 2.

Fig. 3: Comparison of data encoding strategies on BreastMNIST. The MCTS-derived 2×2 circuit is compared against baseline encodings using RX, RY , and higher-order gates with 1 to 3 re-uploading layers; only the best-performing configuration for each baseline is shown. The two angle encodings (RX and RY ) achieve identical performance and therefore overlap. Results are averaged over five random seeds, with error bars indicating standard deviation.

Fig. 6: Feature maps produced by 2×2 patch-sized quantum encodings on BreastMNIST. Circuits C1, C400, and C700 represent the best, medium, and worst performing encodings from the search, respectively. Higher-ranked circuits produce more diverse feature representations.

Fig. 7: Entanglement capability versus validation AUC of various encoding circuits for the BreastMNIST (blue) and the PneumoniaMNIST (orange) datasets. Entanglement values are averaged over three input parameters, with error bars indicating standard deviation.

Fig. 9: Normalized effective rank against validation AUC for candidate encodings on both datasets. A higher effective rank indicates greater feature map diversity. The effective rank is computed on a subset of 20 images, with error bars indicating standard deviation. Pearson correlation: r = 0.87 (BreastMNIST) and r = 0.82 (PneumoniaMNIST).
